item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that I noticed is that both 125-DE and 4918-4 claim to be Barbarossa, which is a bit of a collision, but apart from that, it's great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This takes place in WWI, as noted by Kaiser Wilhelm and the dates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting read. I love they way you put the historical details. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised the Sleeping Giants didn't try to unite under some form of council or gathering to end the madness that was left.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh please tell me you’re a Fate Grand Order player.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was alright, also Finland had an armistice with Nazi germany know your facts. And they were Fascist too you know..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Strong +1!

I truly wish I could read a whole novel about the various Sleeping Heroes. Or a whole series! This entry feels like a fantastic teaser to something much bigger. Awesome awesome work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure why someone would summon Francis Drake considering the English already have Arthur and he's the notorious to the Spanish. Imagine Spain joining the Central Powers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking to myself "hey, this sounds like that one OSP video" and then I read Ihp's author post. I see you have good taste in youtube channels.

item.title: hol up
item.content:encoded: Is that a motherfucking UNDERTALE REFERENCE??!!?!!111 /s +1 is good, take my upvote.

I love this so much. I may be biased as a history and folklore buff but I think this may be one of my favorite articles on the site. The bit about there being more could lead into some extremely interesting tales. Such as Vytautas the Great for Lithuania and Ghengis Khan for Mongolia. Stuff like that could be extremely cool. Now I want to write those, perhaps some day I will.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1.

very nice (tm)

All jokes aside, I think this SCP was brilliantly done. The comment about "ungentlemanly warfare" is quite true nowadays, not just in reference to that one division of the SAS. WW1 was really the last gentleman's war. Anyhow; this was well researched, and I think you did a very good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple concept but handled really well, and it makes for a really fun SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yess!!! I love it. I have seen too many return of Arthur stories shot down. This is brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a beautiful, beautiful story. +1

Mythical stories always resonate very hard with me - and the moment I realized who 4918-1 was, I was completely hooked. The clash between mythic ideals and the modern banality of evil was very, very effective.

I was unaware of Väinämöinen and knew of the Kalevala only in passing before reading this - does anyone know of a good English translation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is because I cannot math properly. The values of their weight have been adjusted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing, lovely eye-catching writing style as well.
Also, why the hell these people weigh upwards of four hundred pounds?
ABSOLUTE U N I T.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: was fun to read, i love your works! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I truly, truly truly loved this article for a lot of reasons

Historically it's an absolute delight to read, and I heartily give it a +1

The only thing is that the name is the same name as Asgore's theme from Undertale so I went through the entirety of the article thinking "fingers in his ass, fingers in his ass…

Still really great though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic work man. I've actually been working on a similar concept (legendary rulers frozen in time until a great need) for a long time, though I think your version is probably better than what I had. Considering the number of very similar irl legends on the subject the only surprise is that no one's done this sooner.

The main difference between mine and this was a particular focus on Constantine XI (who according to a legend from the siege, was frozen in gold beneath the city until the time came for the empire to rise again- similar stuff to Barbarossa and Arthur), so I can probably spin that off into a skip of its own or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The general disgust of the heroes upon seeing the grotesque meatgrinder that was modern warfare was also

In retrospect, this is the highlight of the piece for me. Seeing the different ways that they reacted to the horrible illogic of the Great War both as people and as representatives of their nations was really interesting; Frederick takes the dispassionate view (or as dispassionate as the insanity of the trenches will allow him) making to improve his nation, Arthur runs headfirst into Dodge regardless of possible injury to himself, and so on. WW1 was a perfect setting for this piece; I think that setting it thirty years later would actually actively work against the narrative, considering how the war against Hitler was one of the few times modern warfare has ever even approximated mirroring the black and white conflict of national myth. WW1 is the archetypical industrial-scale human slaughterhouse of the 20th century: devoid of excuse, justice, or proportional motivation. Putting that in perspective to our heroes' histories is quite compelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thoroughly enjoyed this! A lot of historical detail, handled well. The idea of Kaiser Bill sending soldiers to shoot the ravens was entirely believable. The general disgust of the heroes upon seeing the grotesque meatgrinder that was modern warfare was also.

item.title: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded:
So I had this idea about a week ago, just before the 100th anniversary of the Armistice. It wasn't originally going to be a World War I piece, but The Great Hippo made a few good arguments about why it should be WWI and not WWII, among which was the fact that Finland was an allied country, and I think that any Finnish SCP Foundation readers would have their eyebrows raised if Väinämöinen was allied with the Nazis. Same with Frederick I— he was really progressive for his day, and would probably have hated Hitler.

This is also based, in part, on Overly Sarcastic Productions's video on Fionn Mac Cumhaill, specifically the segment where Red discusses how the apocalypse is going to be a lot more interesting depending on how many King in the Mountain/Sleeping Hero myths are true.

Thanks to all of the beta readers for this, including:

Meserach
DrChandra
Weryllium
Researcher Blasko
RailroadRider
Kakroom

And special thanks to The Great Hippo for advice on Frederick's characterization.

Image Attribution:

First image is from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:British_cavalry_resting_1917.jpg

Second image is from here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Inha_runonlaulajat.jpg

Both images are in the public domain.

Hope you enjoy!
